Career flexibility without being pigeonholed
Effective collaboration between engineering, sales, and product
Validated features that meet real user needs
Streamlined processes that empower rather than hinder

pain_points.signal: Communication overhead and cross-team coordination becoming burdensome at scale.
Lack of effective bridge/translation between technical and non-technical stakeholders, leading to superficial adoption of frameworks.
Top-down implementation of processes without understanding team context or real problems.
Agile/Scrum reduced to empty ceremonies and jargon, losing original purpose and value.
Missing role (e.g., Product Owner, onsite customer) to represent business context, causing disconnect between team and business goals.
Scrum Masters focus on enforcing rituals rather than understanding system issues like cycle time or feedback loops.
Work and technical quality (architecture, testing) are invisible to management, while only ceremonies are visible.
Management cannot make promises to customers when work is not visible and predictable.

desired_outcomes.outcome: Make work visible and predictable to management and stakeholders.
Have a dedicated role (Product Owner/onsite customer) to bring business context inside the team.
Enable bottom-up, team-led process adoption that fits actual problems.
Measure and improve cycle time from decision to deploy and feedback from customers.
Focus on real impediments and blockers rather than just implementing ceremonies.
Understand what customers and users actually need through frequent, non-sales contact.
Have clear prioritization of customer requirements for each release.

purchase_triggers.trigger: Need to manage finances with comprehensive transaction downloading and long-term projections.

purchase_triggers.context: Impulsive need for immediate formatting help and deep features.

purchase_triggers.context: Returning to McAfee after trying others, valuing team size and support.

objections.objection: Agile/Scrum has become meaningless jargon and empty ceremonies, not solving real problems.
Top-down process implementation ignores team context and creates friction.
Lack of a Product Owner or business representative leads to disconnection from business goals.
Forced upgrades with minimal new features, seen as revenue-generating rather than value-adding.
Product blames other parties for issues (e.g., Netflix vs. Amazon), leaving problems unresolved.
Inability to edit scorecards after games or sync across devices efficiently.
Budgeting features are rigid and require excessive manual entry, not fitting user workflows.
Vendor lock-in with no viable competitor, creating feeling of being trapped.

switching_triggers.threshold: Insufficient support and functionality.

switching_triggers.threshold: Superior depth and support in critical situations.

deal_breakers: Top-down imposition of processes without understanding context

pain_points.signal: Technical issues not resolved by support, leading to abandonment
Excessive process overhead from management (e.g., Scrum ceremonies) that feels unnecessary and slows down work
Lack of visibility/transparency into progress causing management to impose control measures
Outdated or incomplete data in the product
Hidden costs, unclear pricing, and unexpected charges during setup or usage
Lack of support for existing data import or compatibility issues when switching from another product
Content or features locked behind aggressive paywalls, making the product feel exploitative
Lack of innovation or new content for long-term users, leading to boredom and repetitive tasks
Poorly implemented or mismatched process (e.g., applying Scrum to a discovery-phase team)
Disconnection issues in online play due to internet problems

desired_outcomes.outcome: Effective and responsive technical support that resolves issues quickly
Process that adds value and improves predictability without excessive overhead, tailored to team needs
Clear visibility and communication between team and leadership to build trust
Up-to-date, accurate data and regular updates to maintain product relevance
Smooth onboarding, including data import and transparent pricing
A product that balances monetization with fair access, avoiding aggressive paywalls
Continuous innovation and new features to keep long-term users engaged
Customization options and flexibility in gameplay or usage to suit individual preferences
User-friendly interface and intuitive process flows that reduce learning curve
Reliable connectivity and performance in online or multiplayer features

pain_points.signal: Scrum master role becoming a 'ceremony police' rather than a facilitator, making processes feel like drudgery
Ceremonies are seen as blockers to productivity, consuming too much time and preventing actual work
Unreliable software that stops working without warning, causing data backup failures
Outdated and inaccurate maps in navigation software, leading to navigation errors
Poor user interface design that is unintuitive, inconsistent, or antiquated
Organizations adopting agile in name only ('framework theater') without real cultural buy-in
Lack of proper support or tech support that is expensive and ineffective
Role confusion between scrum master and functional manager, leading to inefficiency
Software not being cross-platform, limiting adoption in certain environments
Monetization tactics that feel pushy, like requiring extra payments for forms or features

desired_outcomes.outcome: Agile processes that are truly adaptive and owned by the team, not just ceremonial
Reliable, 'set-it-and-forget-it' software that works consistently without manual intervention
Intuitive and user-friendly interfaces that require minimal learning curve
Accurate and up-to-date data (e.g., maps, POIs) in software products
Cross-platform compatibility to work across different operating systems and devices
Effective tech support and troubleshooting resources when issues arise
Ability to customize and adapt tools to fit specific workflow needs
Clarity in roles (e.g., scrum master vs. manager) to avoid overlap and confusion
Fair licensing and installation policies without restrictive activation limits
Software that helps practice real-life skills (e.g., blackjack for casino practice)

Excessive time required for basic reporting tasks

Lack of support for collaborative workflows
